title: Predecessors
lean_explanation:
The file formalises the LN definition of *predecessors* of a vertex $v$ under a strict total order on a CDMG, exposing two operators: `Pred` (strict) and `PredLE` (non-strict).

`Pred` takes three arguments after the universe parameters. `G : CDMG Node` is the ambient CDMG over a carrier type `Node : Type*`, with `[DecidableEq Node]` inherited from `def_3_1`. `lt : Node → Node → Prop` is the strict order — a *raw* binary relation, not a `[LT Node]` typeclass and not a structure field, mirroring the parameter convention of `def_3_8`'s `IsTopologicalOrder`. `v : Node` is the focal vertex, deliberately *not* required to lie in $J \cup V$ (following the LN's literal stance). The output is `Set Node`, built by set-builder `{w | w ∈ G ∧ lt w v}`. The conjunct `w ∈ G` is not custom notation: it resolves through the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2` (`CDMGNotation.lean`), which unfolds `w ∈ G` to `w ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`. So the Lean body reads exactly as the LN's $\{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\}$.

`PredLE G lt v` is `G.Pred lt v ∪ {v}` — the strict predecessor set unioned with the singleton $\{v\}$, *not* the reflexive-closure form $\{w \mid w \le v\}$. Lean's elaborator resolves the brace notation `{v}` to `Set.instSingleton` because the left operand of `∪` is `Set Node`, so no explicit ascription is needed; the `∪` is `Set.union`.

The ambient `[DecidableEq Node]` instance is load-bearing for the body: under the `Membership` instance, `w ∈ G` reduces to `Finset.mem` on `G.J ∪ G.V`, which needs decidable equality. Both operators are plain `def`s with no automatic unfolding, so `Set.mem_setOf`, `Set.mem_union`, and `Set.mem_singleton_iff` are the standard destructors at use sites. Downstream chapters reach for `Pred` in CBN factorisation, do-calculus, and σ-/d-separation arguments, and for `PredLE` in iSCM recursions that condition on the values determined up to and including $v$.
design_choices:
**The strict order is passed as a raw `lt : Node → Node → Prop`, not via a `[LT Node]` typeclass.** A `[LT Node]` instance would pin a single canonical strict order to the *type* `Node`, but the LN reads "let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$" — the order is a *parameter* attached to the definition, not a structural feature of nodes. Mirroring `IsTopologicalOrder` (`def_3_8`), `Pred G lt v` reads on the nose as the LN's "predecessors of $v$ in $G$ under $<$", and every consumer plugs in whichever relation they need (typically one carrying a `G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` hypothesis, but `Pred` does not require it).

**The focal vertex `v` is left unconstrained — there is no `(hv : v ∈ G)` hypothesis.** The LN does not constrain $v$ either, and an explicit hypothesis would force every downstream call site to supply a witness even when the vertex is structurally guaranteed to come from the carrier. The corner case $v \notin J \cup V$ is well-defined: the strict body is vacuously empty (the `lt` we ever plug in only relates nodes inside the carrier), and `PredLE` degenerates to `{v}` via the adjoined singleton.

**Output type `Set Node`, not `Finset Node` or the subtype `↥(G.J ∪ G.V)`.** The chapter's family-set primitives (`Pa`, `Ch`, `Sib`, `Anc`, `Desc`, …) all live in `Set Node`, and downstream uses freely intersect predecessor sets with family sets (e.g. $\mathrm{Pred}^G_<(v) \cap \mathrm{Pa}^G(v)$ in do-calculus). A `Finset` carrier would thread decidability of `lt w v` through every call site; a subtype carrier would force `Subtype.val` coercions against ambient `Set Node` quantities, and would make the singleton `{v}` in `PredLE` ill-typed in the corner case $v \notin J \cup V$.

**The `w ∈ G` conjunct lives in the set-builder body, not as a downstream intersection.** Keeping `w ∈ G` inside the body preserves the literal LN reading $\{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\}$ and makes destructuring `h : w ∈ Pred G lt v` as `⟨hw_mem, hw_lt⟩` deliver `hw_mem : w ∈ G` immediately, matching the precedent set by `Pa`, `Ch`, `Sib`.

**`PredLE` uses the *literal* LN body `Pred lt v ∪ {v}`, not the reflexive closure `{w | w ≤ v}` or `{w | lt w v ∨ w = v}`.** The LN's $\le$ subscript names the non-strict variant, but the body it actually writes is the strict body unioned with a singleton. The two forms agree when $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$) and diverge only in the corner case $v \notin J \cup V$. Crucially, the adjoined `{v}` carries no `v ∈ G` guard, so `v ∈ PredLE G lt v` *unconditionally* — even when `v` lies outside the node set. A consumer who prefers the cleaner reflexive-closure form can prove the equivalence in one step under `v ∈ G` and irreflexivity of `lt`.
